title: Comparison & Decision Framework
objectives: Compare Provider / Bloc / Cubit / Riverpod / GetX / Redux / MobX
Pick the right tool based on team, project, timeline, and complexity
Answer 'which state management do you use?' in interviews without being religious about it
Understand the three-tier model: ephemeral / app / server state

sections.markdown:
**Think of state management libraries as woodworking tools in your workshop.**

- **`setState`** is the hand chisel — precise, cheap, right for detail work you do once. Building furniture with only a chisel is torture; refusing to touch a chisel because you have a table saw is silly.
- **Provider** is the reliable hand plane — smooth, well-understood, teaches you the grain of the wood (InheritedWidget). A generation of Flutter devs grew up with it.
- **Riverpod** is the modern power planer — same job, faster, safer, no `BuildContext` scraping the surface.
- **Bloc / Cubit** is the CNC router — expensive setup, staggeringly precise output. Overkill for a shelf; essential for a mass-produced kitchen.
- **GetX** is the multi-tool with 47 attachments — you can build anything, but veterans watch you use it and wince.
- **Redux** is the industrial mill — designed for a factory floor, imported from React's factory town, quiet in Flutter today.
- **MobX** is the reactive band saw — cuts along the grain automatically. Elegant, requires learning its rhythms and paying the codegen tax.

**None of these are wrong.** They're tools with different personalities. The senior engineer picks based on team, project scope, and timeline — not brand loyalty. This chapter gives you that decision framework.

**State management decision** is the discipline of matching a library's trade-offs to your project's constraints — team size, timeline, complexity, testability needs, existing conventions. The goal isn't to find the 'best' library; it's to make a defensible choice you can explain, live with, and ship. This chapter provides the framework for that choice.
## The three tiers — start here

rows: Form field text, expanded flag, animation controller
User profile, cart, theme, feature flags
API results, live streams, cached DB queries

sections.markdown: **The senior answer** to 'which state management do you use?' — **'It depends on the tier. Ephemeral state stays with setState. Shared app state and server state go through a proper library — I default to Riverpod for new apps, but I've shipped production Bloc and Provider codebases too.'** No religion; project-fit thinking. That answer alone signals 3+ YoE.
## The seven-library comparison

rows: Simple, official baseline, easy learning curve
BuildContext-tied, runtime `ProviderNotFoundException`, no built-in async model
Learning curve if new to reactive patterns
Strict unidirectional flow, event audit log, `blocTest`, transformers for debounce/cancel
Large teams; audit / compliance apps; complex event flows
No event audit trail; harder to add transformers later
Simpler feature-scoped state within Bloc-adopting teams
All-in-one (state + nav + DI + snackbars); fastest to write
Global registry; opinionated; fights Flutter idioms; controversial
Time-travel debugging; ported audit-heavy React apps
Heavy ceremony (actions/reducers/middleware); rare in Flutter today
Porting a Redux React app; strict compliance
Elegant automatic reactivity; familiar to Vue/Angular devs

sections.markdown: ## The decision flowchart

sections.code:
START — 'What kind of state?'
 │
 ├─ Widget-local only? ──────────────────→ setState ✅ done.
 │
 └─ Shared across widgets / async / server:
     │
     ├─ Team already uses X (Provider/Bloc/GetX/MobX)?
     │       └─ Yes → KEEP X. Don't rewrite; not worth the churn.
     │
     └─ Greenfield project:
         │
         ├─ Solo / hackathon / freelance / <2 weeks?
         │       └─ GetX is defensible for velocity.
         │
         ├─ Large team / audit-heavy / event-log needs?
         │       └─ Bloc + Cubit (mix for feature-scoped simplicity).
         │
         ├─ From React/Vue/Angular background?
         │       └─ MobX feels familiar.
         │
         └─ Otherwise (the 80% case) → Riverpod ✅.

Rule: 'What's the smallest tool that solves my problem?'
Rule: 'Do I need to explain this choice to a new hire in one paragraph?'
If yes to both — you've picked well.

sections.markdown: ## Migration paths — when they actually make sense

rows: State crosses two+ widgets and prop drilling gets ugly
Adding a new large feature; Provider is limiting you
Team wants to simplify; not urgent

sections.markdown: **A rewrite is almost never worth it on its own.** Migrate WHILE adding a new feature that would benefit from the new library. That way the effort has a business justification, and you contain the risk to the new feature. Big-bang state-management rewrites are one of the most common productivity sinkholes in Flutter shops.
## Interview cheat-sheet — quotable one-liners

sections.code:
'When would you pick X over Y?'
 │
 Riverpod vs Provider:
   'Riverpod is Provider without BuildContext + with compile-safety and
    AsyncValue baked in. Same author. Riverpod for new apps.'

 Riverpod vs Bloc:
   'Riverpod is more ergonomic — less boilerplate. Bloc gives you the event
    audit trail. Big teams that value strict conventions still pick Bloc.'

 Bloc vs Cubit:
   'Same package. Cubit for simple state; Bloc when you need transformers
    (debounce, restartable) or named-event logs. They coexist happily.'

 Anything vs GetX:
   'GetX ships everything in one package. Fast to write, hard to test at scale.
    Fine for hackathons and freelance; large teams outgrow it.'

 Redux in Flutter:
   'Rare — Bloc gives similar discipline with less ceremony. Redux fits when
    porting a React app or under strict audit requirements.'

 MobX:
   'Elegant reactive model, requires codegen. Feels familiar coming from
    Vue/Angular. Riverpod covers most needs without codegen.'

sections.markdown: ## Common mistakes across libraries
**Mistake 1: Picking based on GitHub stars.** Popularity is a signal, not a rule. What matters is fit to YOUR project and team.
**Mistake 2: Rewriting a working codebase.** The number of Flutter projects that shipped with 'let's migrate from Provider to Riverpod first' → never shipped anything is embarrassing.
**Mistake 3: One-size-fits-all.** Even within one app, use `setState` for local UI + a library for shared state. Don't put every checkbox in Riverpod.
**Mistake 4: Ignoring the team's existing skills.** A team fluent in Bloc will ship faster in Bloc than in the trendiest new library. Skills > frameworks.

**Mistake 5: Skipping the Repository pattern.** All libraries benefit from separating server state (Repository) from UI state (state library). Skipping this couples network to widgets — the pain grows exponentially.
## Best practices — universal
**1. Always separate the three tiers.** setState for ephemeral, library for app, Repository + library for server.
**2. Immutable state + copyWith / Freezed / Equatable.** Mutations must produce new state objects, else equality checks skip legitimate rebuilds.
**3. Narrow subscriptions.** `.select` (Riverpod), `Selector` (Provider), `BlocSelector` (Bloc) — rebuild only when the field you care about changes.
**4. Repository owns server state.** Blocs/Notifiers observe it. Never Retrofit calls from a Bloc directly.
**5. Test state without widgets.** `ProviderContainer` (Riverpod), `blocTest` (Bloc), plain Dart unit tests (MobX, GetX). If you can only test with `pumpWidget`, your architecture has drift.
**6. Adopt code-gen where the ecosystem supports it.** `@riverpod`, `@freezed` — a few extra seconds on save saves hours of hand-written boilerplate.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — Choose for 3 scenarios.** Write down which library you'd pick for: (a) a hackathon shopping app, (b) a banking transaction app, (c) an internal analytics dashboard. Justify each in 1 sentence.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Same feature, two libraries.** Build a counter in setState AND Riverpod. Compare LOC, testability, mental effort.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Migrate one screen.** Take a Provider-based screen from a real project; port to Riverpod. Note every surprise.

**Practice 4 (Hard) — Write a 200-word Architecture Decision Record (ADR).** 'This project uses X because…'. Include team background, project scope, timeline, alternatives considered. This IS the senior deliverable.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Mixed architecture.** Design an app that uses BOTH Bloc (for auth flow with named events) AND Cubit (for simple screen state). Draw the boundary clearly.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: Which state management do you use and why?**

**A:** 'It depends on the tier. For ephemeral widget-local state I use `setState` — no ceremony. For app-shared state and server state, I default to **Riverpod** for new projects because it's compile-safe, works without `BuildContext`, has `AsyncValue` built-in, and tests without pumping widgets. I've shipped production apps in **Bloc** too — its stricter event-driven discipline suits large teams and audit-heavy domains. For legacy Provider codebases I keep them on Provider; the rewrite is rarely worth it. Rule: pick based on team, project scope, and timeline — not framework fashion.' — This answer signals seniority without being religious.

**Q2: What are the three tiers of state?**

**A:** **(1) Ephemeral / UI state** — belongs to one widget: form field text, expanded/collapsed flag, animation progress. Managed with `setState`. **(2) App / shared state** — multiple widgets need it: user profile, cart, theme, feature flags. Managed with Riverpod / Bloc / Provider. **(3) Server / remote state** — comes from network / DB; the server holds the truth. Cached locally to feel fast. Managed via Repository + Riverpod's `AsyncNotifier` or Bloc's async event handlers. **Getting the tiers right matters more than which library.** Putting server state in `setState` re-fetches every rebuild; putting ephemeral state in a global store creates ugly coupling.

**Q3: How would you migrate a Provider codebase to Riverpod?**

**A:** Feature-by-feature, WHILE adding new functionality that would benefit from Riverpod's ergonomics. Steps: **(1) Wrap the app in `ProviderScope`** at the root — coexists with Provider fine. **(2) Convert one screen's model** from `ChangeNotifier` → `Notifier`, its provider from `ChangeNotifierProvider` → `NotifierProvider`, its widget from `Consumer<T>` → `ConsumerWidget` + `ref.watch/read`. **(3) Test the converted screen thoroughly.** **(4) Repeat.** **Don't do a big-bang rewrite** — the pattern is nearly 1:1 (same author) but every screen has some subtle context around `Provider.of` that needs review. The migration justifies itself faster if you're already touching that code for a new feature.

**Q4: When would you use plain `setState` in a real app?**

**A:** For widget-local UI state that no other widget needs to know: text-field input while typing, an expanded/collapsed panel, a hover state, an animation controller's progress, a modal's dismissed flag. Anything where the state's LIFETIME matches ONE widget. Overusing `setState` for cross-widget state creates callback-drilling hell; underusing it for local UI state adds ceremony that helps no one. **Rule:** if the state lives in one widget forever and no one outside needs it, `setState`. Anything shared → library.

**Q5: What's the role of a Repository in state management?**

**A:** A Repository owns the single source of truth for a data type (User, Order, Product). It combines multiple data sources — Retrofit/Dio API, local Room/Isar/Hive cache, in-memory cache — and exposes ONE clean async API to the state layer (`Stream<User> watchUser(id)`, `Future<void> refresh()`, `Future<void> update(user)`). The state library (Bloc / Riverpod / whatever) OBSERVES the Repository, translates its data into UI state. **Why crucial:** (1) testable — swap the real Repo with a fake in tests without touching state code. (2) reusable — multiple state layers share one Repo. (3) separation of concerns — state is transient UI logic; data storage is domain logic. **This layer is library-agnostic** — it works with any state solution.

**Q6: What are common state-management anti-patterns?**

**A:** **(1) Prop drilling** — passing callbacks 10 levels deep because you refuse to use a state library. **(2) Everything in a global store** — including UI-local checkbox toggles that don't need to be shared. **(3) Skipping the Repository** — coupling Retrofit calls directly to Blocs / Notifiers. **(4) One giant reducer / notifier / bloc** for the whole app — split by feature. **(5) Not testing state** — if state changes are only verified via widget tests, your architecture has widget-coupling drift. **(6) Rewriting from scratch to adopt the trendy new library** — an unshipped app teaches nobody. **(7) Zealous framework advocacy** — 'Riverpod is objectively best' or 'Bloc is the only serious choice' — both are wrong; both are tools.

**Q7: Can you use multiple state libraries in one app?**

**A:** Yes, and sometimes should. Common patterns: **(1) Cubit for simple feature state + Bloc for complex event-driven flows** — same package, coexist trivially. **(2) Provider for a legacy feature + Riverpod for new work** — perfectly fine while migrating; wrap the whole app in `ProviderScope`, keep the old Provider tree working. **(3) `setState` for local UI + Riverpod for shared state** — everyone does this whether they say so or not. **What to avoid:** picking three libraries because 'why not' — every additional dep is training cost for new engineers and one more thing to keep up-to-date. Rule: use multiple libraries when there's a REASON, not by accident.

**Q8: How do you decide what to put in the state vs computed at build time?**

**A:** Put in state: **user intent** (what the user has done), **data from the server**, **long-lived UI decisions** (selected filter, sort order). Compute at build: **derived views** of state (`state.items.length`, `state.items.where(unpaid)`), **theme colors**, **format strings**. **Rule:** state is what you'd save + restore across app kill. Derived values shouldn't be stored — they can always be recomputed. **Corollary:** for reactive libraries, use `@computed` (MobX), `AsyncValue.select` (Riverpod), or `BlocSelector` for derived views so the derivation stays close to the state.

## Real-world usage across the industry

- **New Flutter apps in 2024–26** — Riverpod is the modern default; Bloc a strong runner-up.
- **Enterprise / banking / healthcare** — Bloc, for the audit trail and strict discipline.
- **Solo indie / freelance / tutorials** — GetX or Riverpod.
- **Wonderous, Now In Android (Kotlin equivalent), reference apps** — often Bloc.
- **Legacy Flutter apps 2019–2022** — Provider dominates.
- **Alibaba, TenCent, Chinese Flutter ecosystem** — heavy MobX + GetX presence.
- **Google internal Flutter** — mostly Bloc + Repository pattern.

## Advanced notes

- **Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)** — document 'we chose X because…' in the repo; onboarding gold.
- **Feature flags for gradual migration** — one screen at a time on the new library.
- **Testing patterns are universal** — Repository + fake DataSources beats mocking a state library.
- **`InheritedNotifier` / `InheritedModel`** — sometimes the right answer is a small custom InheritedWidget; not everything needs a library.
- **`ValueNotifier` + `ValueListenableBuilder`** — Flutter's built-in mini state store; underrated for one-off state.
- **`Riverpod + go_router` together** — the modern default stack for new Flutter apps.
- **`Bloc + get_it + go_router` together** — the enterprise stack.
- **`GetX` all-in-one** — its own stack.
- **Migration ADRs** — write the 'here's why we chose to migrate' doc before you start; forces clarity of thought.

flashcards.back: Riverpod. Bloc a strong second for big teams.
Need event transformers (debounce/restartable) or named-event audit log.
Solo devs, hackathons, freelance, tutorials. Not big teams.
Rare — porting a React app or strict compliance / audit needs.
Never a big-bang rewrite. Migrate WHILE adding a feature that benefits from the new library.

quiz.q: Rewriting from Provider to Riverpod on day 1?
options: Usually not worth it — migrate while adding a new feature
quiz.explain: Big-bang rewrites are a productivity sinkhole.

options: Single source of truth for a data type; combines API + cache; state layer observes it
